planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in summer or divide established crowns in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Thunberg's bush clover dies back to woody crowns each winter and may appear dead long into spring before new growth emerges — do not prune until new growth confirms the crown is alive. It grows very rapidly each spring. The cultivar Gibraltar is the most widely grown, with particularly deep rose-purple flowers.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Thunberg's bush clover is one of the most spectacular late-season perennials available, producing dense, cascading wands of small, rosy-pink to rose-purple pea flowers in August and September — when almost nothing else in the garden is blooming with this abundance — on long, gracefully arching branches that create a weeping, fountain-like form of exceptional elegance. Each individual stem carries hundreds of small flowers in a dense, continuous spike, and a single established plant produces dozens of such stems that cascade toward the ground under their own weight, creating a foamy, waterfall effect of pink bloom that can span 5-7 feet. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it improves soil over time. Native to China and Japan, it dies back to the woody crown each winter and emerges late in spring, growing rapidly to blooming size by midsummer. Extraordinary for filling the late summer gap in the perennial border with a dramatic, long-lasting display highly attractive to bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Sow seeds in lean, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun in early spring or fall; yellow echinacea grows well in lean, dry soils similar to other echinaceas. Cold stratification of seeds for 30-60 days improves germination rates significantly. It is a critical parent species used in breeding yellow and orange hybrid coneflowers and is the only yellow-petaled species in the genus.

directions: Sow seeds in lean, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; yellow echinacea is the botanical paradox of the coneflower genus — named paradoxa because it has bright, clear, lemon-yellow to butter-yellow ray petals that droop downward from the central cone, unlike every other Echinacea species which has pink, purple, or white petals. It is the only naturally occurring yellow-flowered species in the genus, native to dry, rocky, limestone glades and cedar barrens of the Ozarks in southern Missouri, northern Arkansas, and eastern Oklahoma. The unusual drooping yellow petals surrounding a prominent dark-orange to brown central cone create a flower quite different in character from the familiar purple coneflower, with a more elegant, crane-like appearance. As a parent species, it is of enormous horticultural importance — virtually all the yellow, gold, and orange hybrid coneflowers now widely sold (Sunrise, Harvest Moon, Art's Pride) have E. paradoxa as a major parent. Attracts butterflies, native bees, and goldfinches that harvest the seeds in fall.

planting.note: Divide established plants in spring or fall, or sow seeds in well-drained, lean, neutral to strongly alkaline, chalky or limestone soil in full sun; dropwort is completely distinct from the closely related meadowsweet and queen of the prairie, which require consistently moist soil — dropwort actually prefers dry, calcareous, well-drained conditions and performs poorly in wet soil. The cultivar Multiplex (Flore Pleno) has beautiful double white pompom flowers and is the most widely grown ornamental selection.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, neutral to strongly alkaline, chalky or gravelly soil in full sun; dropwort is the drought-tolerant, lime-loving member of the Filipendula genus — completely unlike the moisture-demanding meadowsweet (F. ulmaria) and queen of the prairie (F. rubra). It grows naturally in the short-grass calcareous meadows and chalk downs of Europe and Asia where it thrives in the thin, alkaline soils and dry summers that defeat most moisture-loving perennials. The beautiful, finely cut, fern-like foliage forms an attractive mound above which creamy-white to pale-pink flower clusters appear in June and July, fragrant and attractive to bees. The wild species has single flowers, but the cultivar Multiplex (also sold as Flore Pleno) has charming double pompom flowers that give the flower clusters a denser, more ornamental quality. The small, tuberous, carrot-like roots were used as food in early European history. Outstanding for chalk gardens, dry limestone borders, and other alkaline, well-drained positions.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or small trees in fall or early spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; western redbud is much more drought-tolerant and sun-demanding than eastern redbud and thrives in the dry summers of California, Nevada, and Arizona where the eastern species would fail. Established plants are highly drought-tolerant and require minimal supplemental irrigation in their native climate range.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; western redbud is the California and Great Basin counterpart of the eastern redbud, native to rocky hillsides, chaparral, and dry canyon walls throughout California, Nevada, Arizona, and Utah. It is a distinctly different plant from eastern redbud in several ways: typically growing as a large, multi-stemmed shrub rather than a single-trunked tree (reaching 10-15 feet rather than 20-30 feet), with round rather than heart-shaped leaves, and substantially higher drought tolerance adapted to the dry-summer Mediterranean climate of the American West. In early spring, the leafless stems are covered in brilliant, vibrant magenta-pink pea flowers identical in form to eastern redbud, creating a breathtaking display. The round, deep-green summer leaves have attractive fall color. An outstanding California native for xeriscaping, wildlife gardens, and drought-tolerant landscapes, and increasingly valued as a garden plant in the Gulf Coast and Southeast where its heat tolerance is an advantage.

planting.note: Sow seeds on the surface of well-drained, lean, neutral to alkaline soil in fall for spring germination, or in early spring; Olympic mullein is a biennial that forms a large, felted rosette in year one and sends up a spectacular candelabra-branched flower stem in year two. It self-sows abundantly in dry, sunny, gravelly conditions to maintain a permanent colony. Distinct from the silver mullein (V. bombyciferum) in its more dramatically branched candelabra form and slightly less intensely silver foliage.

directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface in lean, very well-drained, neutral to alkaline, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; Olympic mullein is the most dramatically architectural of the large biennials, native to rocky, dry, mountainous regions of Greece and the Balkans. In its first year it forms a large, flat, broadly spreading rosette of large, soft, gray-white woolly leaves. In its second year it sends up a stout, multi-branched, candelabra-like flower stem reaching 6-8 feet, with numerous spreading side branches each densely packed with bright-yellow flowers that open progressively from the bottom upward over many weeks. The overall effect — a massive, symmetrical candelabra of yellow flowers on a central architectural column — is unlike anything else in the biennial or perennial garden and creates a focal point of extraordinary structural impact. Unlike common mullein (Verbascum thapsus), the Olympic mullein produces a genuinely branched, multi-stemmed candelabra rather than a single spike. It self-sows freely in dry, gravelly conditions to create a self-perpetuating colony.
